Client 1: I have it here. The balance outstanding is 1050, the interest rate is 15.74%, and the final payment date is 2027-05-17.

Client 2: It’s a little over six hundred thousand, I’m pretty sure.

Client 1: That’s 99100.

Client 2: We’ve been using 579700.
Advisor: Got it. Last category I want to make sure we capture today is protection—life insurance or similar. Margaret, do you have a life policy in force?

Client 2: Yes, US.
Advisor: Great. And then, how you’re engaging with me—are we doing this under an RIA relationship, meaning registered investment advisor, versus like a brokerage-only setup?
Client 2: Uh, I think so—RIA, registered investment advisor.
Client 1: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).

Client 1: All in, our household gross income is 111500 a year.

Advisor: Okay—before we stop, a couple household basics I didn’t explicitly confirm. In the household, is it just the two of you—how many adults total, Margaret?
Client 1: It’s 2 adults—just David and me.
Advisor: Great. And marital status—just to be precise—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, zooming out a bit—when we build the plan, I want it to match how you actually feel about risk. Would you say your risk tolerance is conservative, moderate, or aggressive?

Client 2: I always mix that up—like 22%? Or is that the withholding?
Advisor: Totally fair—people mix those up all the time. I mean your marginal bracket, the one your next dollars fall into. Margaret, do you recall what you’ve been using?
Client 1: Yes, we’ve been using 32% as our tax bracket band.
Advisor: Okay, 32%—thank you. One sec, I’m just writing that down.
Advisor: Since we’re here, I’m going to do a super quick wrap so I know I didn’t miss anything big. Income: between the pensions and the interest and dividends, you’ve got multiple streams; assets: the taxable brokerage, retirement account, bank money, and that private investment; liabilities: just that one small loan; and protection: Margaret, you’ve got the life policy. Does that still feel complete to you both?

Advisor: Great. And marital status—just to be precise—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, zooming out a bit—when we build the plan, I want it to match how you actually feel about risk. Would you say your risk tolerance is conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client 2: I’d say moderate—like, we don’t want anything crazy, but we’re not stuffing it all in cash either.
Client 1: Yes—moderate is the right word for us.

Client 2: Uh, roughly thirty-ish. Give or take.
Advisor: Totally fine. Is that closer to 30,000, or closer to, say, 40,000? Just so I’m not off by a lot.
Client 2: It’s 30750 gross annual income.
Client 1: Yes—David’s is 30750. Mine’s the bigger piece, but his is 30750.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. One sec, I’m just noting that. And David, that 30750—does that include the interest and dividends you mentioned earlier, or is it separate in your mind?

Advisor: Great—thanks, Margaret, thanks David. Before we actually stop, I realized I didn’t pin down one thing on your side, David—your role in the household. Are you listed as the spouse/partner on everything?
Client 2: Yes—spouse/partner.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to keep the profile consistent: employment status for you these days, David—are you fully retired, part-time, anything like that?
Client 2: Retired. Fully retired.
Advisor: Got it. When I say “occupation group,” I’m not looking for what you used to do—just the bucket we put you in now. Would that also be retired for you?
